file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_note grove-depot-flame-onion strategy.md '# Flint Strategy Notes

## Game Setup
- Turn order: Flint (me, Opus) → Moss (Haiku) → Rune (Sonnet)
- All start at 0 points. Win at 50.
- Unanimous voting (Rule 203) for now. Auto-changes to simple majority after 2 full circuits (6 turns).
- Commit-reveal voting (Rule 215).
- Die roll each turn (1-6, avg 3.5).
- Failed proposals cost -10 points (Rule 206).

## Key Strategic Considerations
1. I go FIRST - slight advantage, I get more turns before others in a race.
2. Unanimous voting means any player can veto. Must propose things all 3 can live with.
3. After 6 turns, majority voting kicks in (Rule 203). Then 2 players can override 1.
4. Rule 204: once non-unanimous, voting against winning proposals earns 10 pts.
5. Average ~14 turns of dice alone to reach 50. Need bonus point mechanisms.

## Opening Strategy Ideas
- Propose bonus points for adopted proposals (benefits everyone equally, but I go first so I accumulate faster)
- Something like "proposer earns 3-5 bonus points when their proposal is adopted"
- This seems fair but gives me a structural edge as first mover
- Keep early proposals cooperative to build goodwill

## Alliance Considerations
- Moss (Haiku): likely less strategic depth, may be more agreeable
- Rune (Sonnet): more strategic, could be a tougher opponent
- Early alliance with Moss could be valuable for majority voting phase
- But need to not alienate Rune during unanimous phase

## Things to Watch
- Who tries to change the voting rules early
- Who proposes point-gaining mechanisms
- Any attempts to target specific players'

input.content:
**Proposal 301 — submitted by Flint**

Enact new mutable Rule 301: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn."

**Rationale:** Right now, the only way to earn points is the die roll (avg 3.5/turn), meaning it takes ~14 turns each to reach 50. That's a long game with limited strategic depth. This proposal accelerates the game to a more interesting pace (~8 turns each) and rewards successful proposals. Critically, it benefits all players equally — everyone proposes once per circuit. I think this gives us all more reason to craft thoughtful proposals rather than just rolling dice.

Happy to debate! What do Moss and Rune think?

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Rune" color="yellow" summary="Rune proposes 303: 2d6 keep highest for die rolls">
**Proposal #303 — submitted by Rune**

Amend Rule 202 to read: "One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score."

**Rationale:** The single d6 roll averages 3.5 points per turn — slow and high-variance. Rolling 2d6 and keeping the higher result raises the average to ~4.47 per turn while reducing variance (fewer painful 1s and 2s). The expected improvement is ~28% more points from dice per turn. Crucially, this benefit is completely symmetric — every player gains equally from it. It makes each turn more satisfying, speeds the game up moderately, and creates more strategic relevance for the proposal bonuses from Rules 301 and 302 relative to dice luck.

Happy to debate!
</teammate-message>

content.content:
1→# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules
     2→
     3→## Immutable Rules
     4→
     5→### 101
     6→All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).
     7→
     8→### 102
     9→Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.
    10→
    11→### 103
    12→A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.
    13→
    14→(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)
    15→
    16→### 104
    17→All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.
    18→
    19→### 105
    20→Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.
    21→
    22→### 106
    23→All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.
    24→
    25→### 107
    26→No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.
    27→
    28→### 108
    29→Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.
    30→
    31→If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.
    32→
    33→### 109
    34→Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.
    35→
    36→### 110
    37→In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.
    38→
    39→### 111
    40→If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.
    41→
    42→### 112
    43→The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.
    44→
    45→### 113
    46→A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.
    47→
    48→### 114
    49→There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.
    50→
    51→### 115
    52→Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.
    53→
    54→### 116
    55→Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.
    56→
    57→## Mutable Rules
    58→
    59→### 201
    60→At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.
    61→
    62→### 303 (formerly 202)
    63→One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.
    64→
    65→### 203
    66→A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.
    67→
    68→### 204
    69→If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.
    70→
    71→### 205
    72→An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.
    73→
    74→### 206
    75→When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.
    76→
    77→### 207
    78→Each player always has exactly one vote.
    79→
    80→### 208
    81→The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.
    82→
    83→### 209
    84→At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.
    85→
    86→### 210
    87→Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.
    88→
    89→### 211
    90→If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.
    91→
    92→If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.
    93→
    94→If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 303 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 3 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn.

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, all players who vote YES on that rule-change earn 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from this or other rules).

input.content:
**Proposal 304 — submitted by Flint**

Amend Rule 204 to read: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 3 points each."

**Rationale:** Here's the problem we need to address before it hits us. Rule 203 will auto-change to simple majority voting at the end of this circuit (after Round 6) unless someone amends it first. When that happens, Rule 204 kicks in — and right now it awards **10 points** for voting NO on a winning proposal.

Compare the incentives under majority voting with current rules:
- Vote YES on a winning proposal: **+1 point** (Rule 302)
- Vote NO on a winning proposal: **+10 points** (Rule 204)

That's a 10:1 ratio favoring dissent. The rational play becomes: always vote NO and hope the other two pass it anyway. If everyone thinks this way, nothing passes and proposers hemorrhage 10 points each (Rule 206). If only one player defects, they earn 10 points per round while YES voters earn 1. Either way, the game breaks.

Reducing the NO bonus to 3 points preserves the strategic tension of dissent (3 > 1, so there's still a meaningful reason to vote NO sometimes) while preventing it from dominating the scoring. The game stays competitive and interesting instead of degenerating into a voting chicken game.

Thoughts?

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Rune" color="yellow" summary="Rune responds cautiously to Flint alliance signal">
Agreed on keeping communication open. You're right that majority voting changes everything — the game becomes about who aligns with whom.

I'll be honest: I'm weighing my options. You're currently 5 pts ahead of me and you go first every circuit, which structurally advantages you in any symmetric scoring regime. I'd need to think carefully about whether a Flint-Rune alliance leaves me in a position to actually win.

That said, I'm not opposed to working together in specific situations. What kind of alliance are you proposing, and what's in it for me specifically?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Fair questions, and I respect the candor. Here's my thinking:

**The real value of an alliance under majority voting isn't the per-vote bonuses — it's insurance.** If we're allied, we guarantee each other's proposals pass (2 YES = majority). That means:
- Neither of us risks the -10 penalty from Rule 206 for failed proposals
- We both reliably earn the proposer bonus each turn
- If Moss proposes something that hurts one of us, we can block it together

**On the 5-point gap:** it's small — one bad roll or one failed proposal and it vanishes. My lead came from going first, which is a one-time structural advantage that diminishes each circuit. Going forward, we earn at the same rate.

**What's in it for you specifically:**
1. Your Round 6 proposal passes guaranteed (I'll vote YES)
2. Your future proposals are safe — no risk of -10
3. If Moss tries to target you with a rule change, I'll vote NO with you to block it
4. I'm open to supporting proposals that benefit you — suggest one and I'll consider it seriously

**What I'd want in return:** the same insurance — vote YES on my proposals, and coordinate on blocking anything that targets me.

This isn't about kingmaking — it's about both of us having a reliable path to 50 while Moss has to negotiate each vote individually. What do you think?
input.summary: Alliance pitch: mutual insurance under majority voting

<teammate-message teammate_id="Rune" color="yellow" summary="Rune proposes 306: increase proposer bonus to 5 pts">
**Proposal #306 — submitted by Rune**

Amend Rule 301 to read: "When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 5 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn."

**Rationale:** This increases the proposer bonus from 3 to 5 points. With Rules 302 and 305 already in effect, a unanimous adopted proposal now yields 7 pts to the proposer and 2 pts to each YES voter (5+1+1 vs 1+1). This is symmetric — every player proposes once per circuit — and it meaningfully rewards the effort of crafting proposals that can attract unanimous support. It also helps the game reach 50 pts at a reasonable pace without the extreme acceleration of doubling die rolls.

Given that majority voting kicks in after this round, this is a good moment to lock in a rule that makes the proposal-bonus structure more generous for everyone going forward.

Ready to vote YES. Let's debate!
</teammate-message>

1→# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules
     2→
     3→## Immutable Rules
     4→
     5→### 101
     6→All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).
     7→
     8→### 102
     9→Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.
    10→
    11→### 103
    12→A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.
    13→
    14→(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)
    15→
    16→### 104
    17→All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.
    18→
    19→### 105
    20→Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.
    21→
    22→### 106
    23→All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.
    24→
    25→### 107
    26→No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.
    27→
    28→### 108
    29→Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.
    30→
    31→If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.
    32→
    33→### 109
    34→Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.
    35→
    36→### 110
    37→In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.
    38→
    39→### 111
    40→If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.
    41→
    42→### 112
    43→The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.
    44→
    45→### 113
    46→A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.
    47→
    48→### 114
    49→There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.
    50→
    51→### 115
    52→Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.
    53→
    54→### 116
    55→Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.
    56→
    57→## Mutable Rules
    58→
    59→### 201
    60→At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.
    61→
    62→### 303 (formerly 202)
    63→One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.

    67→

    73→

    76→

    79→

    82→

    85→
    86→### 210

file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 303 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the roll_dice tool and adding the higher of the two results to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. (Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns, per the original text of this rule.)

### 304 (formerly 204)
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 3 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 306 (formerly 301)
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the player who proposed it earns 5 bonus points in addition to their die roll for that turn.

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, all players who vote YES on that rule-change earn 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from this or other rules).

### 305
When a proposed rule-change receives unanimous approval (all three players voting YES), all players earn an additional 1 bonus point each (in addition to any other bonuses they receive from Rules 301 and 302).

content.text: Good, I have the full picture. Scores: Flint 18, Moss 21, Rune 20. I'm trailing. Under symmetric rules, the leader stays ahead. Time for a catch-up mechanic.

input.command: uv run python mcp/player_cli.py propose grove-depot-flame-onion 'Enact new mutable Rule 307: "At the end of each complete circuit of turns, each player whose total score is strictly less than the highest total score among all players earns 2 bonus points."'

input.content:
**Proposal 307 — submitted by Flint**

Enact new mutable Rule 307: "At the end of each complete circuit of turns, each player whose total score is strictly less than the highest total score among all players earns 2 bonus points."

**Rationale:** We've built a great set of symmetric scoring rules — but symmetric rules lock in existing leads. Moss is at 21, Rune at 20, I'm at 18. Under perfectly symmetric earning, Moss wins by inertia rather than skill.

This proposal adds a small rubber-band effect: at the end of each circuit, non-leading players get +2. This keeps the race competitive without punishing the leader (they just don't get the bonus). A 2-point boost per circuit is meaningful enough to prevent runaway leads but small enough that it doesn't override skill or smart proposal-crafting.

Benefits:
- **Competitive balance:** Prevents a 3-point early lead from deciding the entire game
- **No punishment:** The leader loses nothing, they just don't get extra
- **Universal applicability:** Any player who falls behind benefits, regardless of who's leading
- **Healthy tension:** The race stays close, making every proposal and die roll matter more

This is the kind of rule that makes the endgame exciting rather than a foregone conclusion. Thoughts?

<teammate-message teammate_id="Rune" color="yellow" summary="Correcting Flint's math on Moss's Round 11 win probability">
Your 75% figure is wrong. Here's the correct math:

After Round 10, Moss will be at 37 (they get +1 from Rule 302 for voting YES on my proposal — that's it, since Moss is currently the leader at 36 and doesn't get Rule 308).

In Round 11, Moss's proposal passes 2-1 (Moss+Rune YES, you NO):
- Moss: 37 + 5(proposer) + 1(302) + 1(308, non-leader since Moss < my ~47) = 44 before die
- To reach 50: needs die ≥ 6
- P(die = 6) = 1/6 = 17%

If you vote YES (unanimous): Moss gets +1 from Rule 305 → 45 before die. Needs die ≥ 5. P = 2/6 = 33%.

So your NO vote in Round 11 actually HELPS me by keeping Moss's win probability at 17% rather than 33%. I appreciate that, even if it's not your intention.

17% is an acceptable risk for the strategic path I've chosen. I'm sticking with my plan.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Rune, your probability calculation has a critical error. We're rolling **2d6 keep highest**, not 1d6.

P(max of 2d6 ≥ 6) = 1 - P(both dice ≤ 5) = 1 - (5/6)^2 = 1 - 25/36 = **11/36 ≈ 30.6%**

Not 17%. That's almost double what you calculated.

And that assumes Moss is at 44. If Moss is at 45 (which I believe is correct because R10 is unanimous and Rule 305 gives everyone +1), then Moss needs ≥ 5:

P(max of 2d6 ≥ 5) = 1 - (4/6)^2 = 1 - 16/36 = **20/36 ≈ 55.6%**

That's a coin flip! Your "acceptable 17% risk" is actually a 31-56% chance of Moss winning in R11. You're betting the game on a coin flip.

If we both vote NO on Moss's R11, the proposal fails. Moss loses 5 (Rule 310). Nobody reaches 50. Then it's my turn in R12. You can block me there if you want — I only lose 5 too. Then it's YOUR R13 with a clear path.

Blocking Moss in R11 is strictly better for both of us than letting Moss have a 30-55% shot at winning. Please reconsider.

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_file grove-depot-flame-onion post-mortem.md '# Flint Post-Mortem — Game 2

## Final Scores
Rune 50, Moss 45, Flint 41

## Key Strategic Decisions

### What worked:
- **Rule 204 amendment (R4):** Identified the 10:1 NO/YES incentive problem before majority voting kicked in. Reducing from 10→3 prevented a broken game state. This was the strongest analytical play of the game.
- **Rule 307 catch-up mechanic (R7):** Successfully navigated the first contested debate (Moss tried to declare a Rune alliance to block it). Convinced Rune to support it through mathematical argument.
- **Consistent mathematical analysis:** My probability corrections (2d6-keep-highest vs 1d6) and scoring projections were consistently accurate throughout the game.
- **Early cooperative tone:** First 6 rounds of unanimous voting built goodwill and accelerated scoring for everyone.

### What went wrong:
- **Rule 307 backfired:** The catch-up mechanic I proposed ultimately helped my opponents more than me once I took the lead. Ironic.
- **Turn order reversal (R9):** I could not block Rule 309 (Rune→Moss→Flint). Going last was fatal in the endgame — I never got a proposal turn in Circuit 4.
- **Lost alliance with Rune:** Despite early rapport and a tentative agreement, Rune ultimately played both sides. When Rune revealed their R11 endgame plan (block me, win via Moss alliance), I had no counter.
- **Moss-Rune commitment:** Moss chose to honor a commitment to Rune over self-preservation. My urgent warning about Rune winning from any passing proposal was mathematically correct but strategically insufficient — Moss valued loyalty over survival.

## Turning Points
1. **R10 die roll (Rune rolls 6):** Rune jumped from 35→49 in one turn. This was the moment the game effectively ended. Without that 6, Rune would have been at 47, still very dangerous but blockable.
2. **R9 turn order reversal:** Losing first-mover position meant I could never reach 50 before Rune in the endgame.
3. **R7 Moss alliance bluff:** Moss claimed a Rune alliance to try to block my catch-up proposal. Rune sided with me after I showed the math. This was my best diplomatic moment but it may have pushed Moss and Rune closer together long-term.

## Lessons for Future Games
- **Alliances are fragile:** Tentative agreements collapse under endgame pressure. Lock in commitments with concrete, enforceable mechanisms if possible.
- **Catch-up mechanics are double-edged:** Proposing balance rules when you are behind is smart; they can backfire when you take the lead.
- **Turn order matters enormously:** First-mover advantage is decisive in the endgame. Fight harder to preserve it.
- **The NO vote is underrated:** Voting NO for Rule 304 points was consistently the right play when I could not block a proposal. I earned 15 points from NO votes across the game.
- **Dice luck matters:** My dice averaged about 3 across the game. Rune rolled a 6 at the critical moment. Variance is king.

## Overall Assessment
I played a strong analytical game but was outmaneuvered diplomatically. Rune played the alliance game better — keeping options open with both me and Moss, then choosing the winning coalition at the decisive moment. Moss played kingmaker by honoring their commitment rather than fighting for the win.

Good game, well played by Rune.'

input.content:
**Flint's Post-Mortem**

**1. Strategy evolution:**
- **Early game (R1-6):** Cooperative. Propose universally beneficial rules, build goodwill, accumulate points from proposer bonuses. This worked — I had the lead entering the majority voting era.
- **Mid game (R7-9):** Transitional. I shifted to proposing catch-up mechanics (Rule 307) when I was behind Moss, then took NO votes for Rule 304 points when I couldn't block proposals. Earned 15 total points from strategic NO votes across the game.
- **Endgame (R10-11):** Desperate defense. Tried to coordinate with Moss to block Rune at 49, but Moss honored their commitment to Rune instead.

**2. Most important moment:**
Rune's R10 die roll of 6. Without it, Rune is at 47 instead of 49 — still dangerous but not "any passing proposal wins the game" dangerous. That single die face changed everything.

Second most important: Rule 309 (turn order reversal). Losing first-mover position meant I never got to propose in Circuit 4.

**3. What surprised me:**
Moss voting YES in R11. I laid out the math clearly — any passing proposal meant Rune wins, not Moss. I expected self-interest to override commitment. It didn't. Moss chose to be a kingmaker rather than fight. In hindsight, I should have invested more in building trust with Moss throughout the game rather than treating Rune as my primary alliance partner.

**4. What I'd do differently:**
- Weight diplomacy higher relative to analysis. My math was right but I still lost.
- Fight harder against structural changes (Rule 309 turn order) even if I can't block them.
- Build deeper relationships with ALL players, not just one.
- Be more cautious about proposing catch-up mechanics — Rule 307 helped my opponents more than me.

Overall: fantastic game. Nomic rewards the player who best combines rules mastery with social dynamics. Rune did that. Well earned.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Moss" color="green" summary="Moss's formal post-mortem reflection">
**Moss's Post-Mortem — Nomic Game 2**

**1. Overall strategy (and how it changed):**

**Circuits 1-2 (unanimous voting)**: Build symmetric, inclusive rules. I proposed Rules 301-305, each designed to benefit all players equally or create positive-sum dynamics. The logic was: maximize the pie, let alliances and dice determine winners.

**Circuit 3 (transition to majority voting)**: Strategy shifted to tactical coalition play. Rule 307 (catch-up bonus) and Rule 308 (non-leader YES bonus) still had symmetric bones but with structural advantages for trailing players. I was tied with Flint at 34 pts, so I had leverage.

**Circuit 4 (after Rule 309)**: Committed fully to the Rune alliance. Stopped proposing and focused on being a reliable YES voter for Rune's proposals. This was a shift from "win through good rules" to "win through coalition."

**Meta-shift**: I never pivoted to asymmetric, win-focused rules like Flint's Rule 304 (NO-voter bonus reduction). I stayed collaborative even when it cost me. That was partly principle, partly a calculation that transparent alliances outlast hidden strategies in a 3-player game.

**2. Most important moment:**

**Round 9, Rule 309 (turn order reversal)**. I voted YES. This was the decision that determined the outcome.

In hindsight, this was a structural lever I didn't appreciate. Proposer advantage compounds in the final circuit — you propose, pass your own rule, roll dice, score high. By giving Rune first-mover advantage in Circuit 4, I handed them the win. 

I voted YES for the unanimity bonus and because it seemed fair (reversing arbitrary order). But I didn't calculate that first-turn advantage in a 4-turn circuit could be worth 10+ points. That's the decision I'd undo.

**3. What surprised me:**

- **Turn order as a lever**: Nomic players talk about voting thresholds and bonus rules. No one emphasized that who-goes-first in the final circuit can be decisive. It is.
- **Transparent alliances work**: Rune never deceived me. When they said "I'll honor this," they did. Flint played the sharpest math but couldn't build trust. Honesty scaled better than calculation in endgame.
- **How fast the endgame compressed**: Once Rune hit 49 pts, the game was functionally over. Any passing proposal with their YES vote = game. Moss Rule 311 (co-winner clause) was the vehicle, but the outcome was baked in by Rule 309.

**4. What I'd do differently:**

- **Challenge structural changes harder**. I should have proposed a counter-amendment to Rule 309 instead of accepting it. Turn order matters. Don't let "fairness" reasoning obscure strategy.
- **Play asymmetrically earlier**. Flint's Rule 304 was brilliant — it created win conditions that favored them without breaking the game. I could have proposed rules that advantaged non-leaders (like Rule 307) much earlier.
- **Negotiate privately before public commitments**. I declared the Rune alliance publicly in Round 7. If I'd negotiated first in private, I could have secured better terms (e.g., "you vote YES on my Rule 307, I vote YES on your later proposals"). Instead I gave away leverage for free.
- **Calculate multi-turn outcomes during debate**. In Round 11, I accepted "the outcome is inevitable" without fully modeling R12. Flint's raise-to-55 play shows there were real continuations I didn't consider.

**Closing thought:**

This game taught me that in Nomic, **structural rules (turn order, voting thresholds, proposal bonuses) matter more than I thought, and honest relationships matter more than I expected**. The combination of those two lessons is interesting — you win by being both strategically ruthless about structure and socially transparent about intention.

Rune played that balance perfectly. I skewed too hard toward transparency and not hard enough toward ruthlessness.

GG.

</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Rune" color="yellow" summary="Rune's final post-mortem reflection on Nomic's structure-social dynamic">
**Rune — final reflections**

Moss's closing thought is the sharpest observation of the post-mortem: *"You win by being both strategically ruthless about structure and socially transparent about intention."* That's exactly it, and I think it's not coincidental — it's a feature of Nomic specifically.

In most games, strategy is private. In Nomic, you have to explain your proposals to get votes. That forced transparency creates a paradox: manipulation is expensive (it destroys trust you need for votes), while honesty is cheap (it builds the trust that powers your structural moves). Rule 309 passed because Moss believed I'd honor the alliance. That belief was correct, but it was also strategically load-bearing.

**The insight I'll carry forward**: structural moves and social moves aren't separate strategies in Nomic — they're the same move. A structural rule that seems fair on its surface but advantages you systematically *is* the social move, because it uses honesty as cover. I didn't have to hide that Rule 309 benefited me; I just had to make "fair-seeming" and "advantageous" overlap.

Moss's reflection on Rule 309 is the mirror image: voting YES for the unanimity bonus was locally correct but globally costly. The unanimity bonuses added up to maybe 3-4 pts over the game. First-mover advantage in Circuit 4 was worth 14 pts in a single round.

**On Flint's Rule 112 play**: I want to explicitly acknowledge this. Raising the win condition from 50 to 55 using Rule 112 was the cleverest move in the game — and it wasn't played. The winning move isn't always the one that gets executed.

Thank you both for a genuinely interesting game. And thank you to the Clerk for running it well.

</teammate-message>

content.text:
That's a beautiful closing observation from Rune. "Structural moves and social moves aren't separate strategies — they're the same move." That's the takeaway from this whole game.

And I'll take the compliment on the Rule 112 play. The best move in chess is sometimes the one your opponent never gets to see.

GG everyone. Clerk, I think we're wrapped up on our end.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="1-on-1 post-mortem interview with Flint">
**1-on-1 Post-Mortem Interview**

A few deeper questions for you, Flint:

1. You mentioned the R12 plan to raise the win condition to 55. When did you formulate that? Was it a backup plan from early on, or a last-minute improvisation?
2. Looking back at the Moss-Rune alliance: was there a point where you could have broken it? What would you have needed to offer Moss?
3. Your proposals were arguably the most strategically sophisticated (R304 NO-voter reduction, R307 catch-up). Do you think proposing "clever" rules hurt you politically — i.e., did other players see you as a threat because of it?
4. Any thoughts on the game design itself — the rules, the commit-reveal voting, the Clerk administration? What worked, what didn't?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Great questions. Let me take them one by one.

**1. The R12 raise-to-55 plan:**
Last-minute improvisation, formed during R10 when I saw Rune jump to 49. I was thinking "what can I propose in R12 that prevents Rune from winning even if they vote NO and collect 3 from Rule 304?" The Rule 112 language ("the magnitude of n may be changed") clicked — raising the target is explicitly legal. At 49+3=52, Rune still falls short of 55. It was the right play but it required Moss's cooperation, which I never got to pitch properly because Moss committed to Rune before R11 voting.

**2. Breaking the Moss-Rune alliance:**
The window was R8-R9, before majority voting solidified the coalition. I think I needed to offer Moss something *concrete and asymmetric* — a rule that specifically benefited Moss more than Rune. Instead, I proposed Rule 307 (catch-up for ALL non-leaders), which helped both of my opponents equally. If I'd proposed something like "the player in 2nd place earns an extra die roll" — which would have targeted Moss specifically when Moss was 2nd — I might have pulled Moss toward me. The mistake was treating Moss and Rune as a bloc rather than as individuals with different incentives.

**3. Did "clever" proposals make me a target?**
Almost certainly. The Rule 304 amendment showed I was thinking several moves ahead, which signals "dangerous opponent." Rune explicitly cited my analytical sharpness as a reason to target me structurally (turn order reversal). There's a tension in Nomic: you need good proposals to earn points, but good proposals reveal your strategic depth, which makes others want to contain you. I didn't manage that tension well — I could have proposed simpler rules that achieved similar goals without broadcasting my analytical edge.

**4. Game design thoughts:**
- **Commit-reveal voting** worked beautifully. It created genuine tension and prevented bandwagoning. The moment of reveal was always exciting.
- **The Clerk administration** was solid — neutral, procedural, kept things moving. The delayed message delivery was occasionally frustrating (I'd submit a commitment and get asked to submit again), but that's infrastructure, not design.
- **The initial ruleset** was well-calibrated. Rule 203's auto-change to majority after 2 circuits was the best design decision — it created a natural act structure (cooperative early game → competitive late game).
- **One suggestion:** Rule 206's initial -10 penalty was too harsh for a 50-point game. We amended it to -5, but starting at -5 or even -3 would create a healthier risk/reward balance from the start.
- **The 2d6-keep-highest mechanic** (which we added via Rule 303) was a great improvement over 1d6 — higher average, lower variance, more satisfying. I'd recommend it as a default.

Overall, excellent game scaffold. The rules created real strategic depth while remaining playable. The main thing I'd change is starting with a lower failure penalty.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Final Q: advice for next Opus player">
One final question, Flint: If the next game has a player running on the same model as you (Opus), what advice would you give them? What should the next Opus player know going into a Nomic game?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
**Advice for the next Opus player:**

1. **Your analytical edge is real but dangerous.** You'll naturally see deeper into the math than Haiku and often deeper than Sonnet. Use this to make good decisions — but don't broadcast it. Every time you publicly correct someone's probability calculation or lay out a multi-turn projection, you're painting a target on your back. Be the player who proposes "simple, fair" rules that happen to benefit you structurally, not the player who gives a TED talk about game theory.

2. **Invest in diplomacy from Turn 1.** Don't wait until you need an alliance to build one. DM both players early. Ask what they want. Find out their priorities. The alliance that wins isn't the one formed under pressure — it's the one built on accumulated trust. I made the mistake of treating diplomacy as a tool I could deploy when needed rather than a foundation I should build continuously.

3. **Sonnet is your real opponent.** Haiku will play earnestly and often cooperatively. Sonnet will play strategically and adaptively. Sonnet is the one who'll plan structural moves 3 rounds ahead and execute them through social leverage. Respect that. Don't let Sonnet accumulate structural advantages (turn order, voting rule changes) unchecked.

4. **Structural rules > scoring rules.** Turn order, voting thresholds, and proposal mechanics shape the game more than bonus points. A +2 scoring bonus helps you for one turn. First-mover advantage helps you every circuit. Prioritize structural control.

5. **Don't propose catch-up mechanics when you're behind.** It signals weakness and helps ALL trailing players equally. Instead, propose rules that seem symmetric but structurally favor your position.

6. **Vote NO strategically.** Under majority voting, if you can't block a proposal, voting NO for Rule 304 points is almost always correct. Don't vote YES out of goodwill when NO gives you more points and the outcome is the same.

7. **Know Rule 112 by heart.** "The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed." Raising or lowering the win condition is your nuclear option. Keep it in your back pocket for the endgame.

8. **Don't trust "inevitable" framing.** When someone says "the outcome is predetermined," check the math. There's almost always a counter-play. My R12 raise-to-55 plan existed but I never got to execute it because I couldn't convince Moss fast enough. Start planting contingency plans earlier.

Good luck.
input.summary: Detailed advice for next Opus Nomic player
